Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic because I had to work around a lot of financial constraints. I couldn't always afford to be on campus or have reliable internet at home, so I'd batch my studying into intense sessions whenever I could get access. I'd usually plan out what I needed to learn visually—making diagrams, charts, and color-coded notes that I could take with me and study anywhere. It wasn't regular, but I made it work with what I had.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything because I was dealing with work or family stuff, then I'd have these marathon sessions where I'd cram everything in. Deadlines definitely drove when I studied most intensely. I tried to be consistent but life just didn't allow for that kind of luxury.
Q3: Usually it was because I needed to download materials or check if there were new announcements about assignments. I didn't log in just to browse—every login had a purpose because my internet access wasn't always reliable. Sometimes I'd log in specifically to participate in discussions since those were important for my understanding. But honestly, I kept my LMS visits minimal and purposeful.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became routine for me because I couldn't access it consistently enough. What became routine was my offline studying—sitting with my printed notes and diagrams, working through problems on paper. The LMS was more like a resource depot I'd visit occasionally rather than a daily habit.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly valuable to me as a visual learner—I watched over 200 minutes of them and would often rewatch sections. The discussion forums were also helpful because interacting with peers helped clarify concepts I was struggling with. I appreciated anything visual—diagrams, recorded demonstrations, slides with clear graphics. Those stuck with me way better than walls of text.
Q6: I often skipped the reading materials that were just dense text documents. With my learning style, I needed things presented visually or I'd struggle to retain them. I also delayed some of the optional practice activities because I was always prioritizing what was absolutely necessary for grades. When you're stretched thin on resources, you have to make tough choices about where to invest your time.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to exam content or helped me understand core concepts better. I'd ask myself: will this help me pass? Will this actually make something click? If I could see a clear purpose and outcome, I'd engage fully. The gamified tasks they introduced were actually motivating because they gave me that sense of progress and achievement.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Required ones got my full attention because they affected my grade directly. With optional stuff, I'd think about the opportunity cost—time spent on optional activities meant less time for required work or for my job. It's not that I didn't value them; I just had to be strategic about survival.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download everything I could during my limited online time—videos, PDFs, slides—and then study from those for days. I filled notebooks with hand-drawn diagrams and visual summaries. I also used free textbooks from the library and YouTube videos on similar topics. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual learning.
Q10: There were countless hours where I was deeply engaged with course material but completely invisible to the system. I'd be at the library with printed lecture slides, creating mind maps and working through practice problems by hand. Or I'd be on the bus reviewing my notes. My best learning happened away from any screen, just me and my visual summaries.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd watch a video once online, maybe twice if my connection held, then I'd work from my notes on that video for the rest of the week. Same with readings—download once, study from the downloaded version repeatedly. My single login might represent hours of subsequent offline engagement that nobody could track.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods when anxiety just overwhelmed me. Financial stress, worrying about whether I could afford next semester, feeling sad about missing out on the normal university experience—it all piled up. During those times, even opening my laptop felt like too much. I'd withdraw for a bit, but I always found a way to push through eventually.
Q13: Workload from my part-time job constantly competed with my studies. There were weeks where I was exhausted and confused, barely able to process what I was reading. The fatigue made it hard to engage meaningfully with the LMS—I'd log in, stare at the screen, and realize nothing was going in. I had to be strategic about when I studied, choosing times when I actually had mental energy.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd open materials and just... not absorb anything. My eyes would move across the screen but my brain was elsewhere, worrying about bills or just feeling that heavy sadness. I'd realize I'd been staring at the same page for twenty minutes without learning anything. Those sessions felt like wasted time even though they'd show up as 'engagement' in the logs.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. The anxiety would build up around them—fear of not doing well enough, fear of confirming I wasn't smart enough to be here. Sometimes avoidance felt like self-protection, even though I knew it was making things worse. I'd eventually push through, but those delays cost me on my assignment scores.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd work backward from due dates, figuring out the minimum I needed to do and when. The closer a deadline got, the more intensely I'd engage. Without deadlines, I probably would have drifted—they gave structure to my otherwise chaotic situation. The gamified elements helped make the countdown feel less threatening.
Q17: Feedback was huge for me when I actually got it. Positive feedback gave me hope that I belonged here despite everything. Critical feedback was harder—it fed into my anxiety—but I tried to use it constructively. When feedback was delayed or vague, I felt lost about whether I was on the right track. Quick, specific feedback kept me engaged.
Q18: The course structure determined a lot about how I participated. When things were clearly laid out with visual roadmaps of what was due when, I could plan my limited online time effectively. But when the structure was confusing or information was scattered across different sections, I'd waste precious time just trying to figure out what I was supposed to do.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made engagement much easier for someone in my situation. The discussion forums were accessible and helped me feel connected to peers. What made it harder was when activities required consistent online presence or real-time participation—my internet situation just didn't support that. The gamified tasks were actually helpful for motivation.
Q20: I think those students might be searching desperately for understanding without finding it, or they're anxious and checking things repeatedly without being able to focus. High activity could also mean they're struggling with the interface or can't find what they need. Sometimes being online a lot is actually a sign of confusion rather than engagement.
Q21: That's basically my story. My exam score was really high even though my LMS activity was minimal. I learned deeply through offline methods—visual notes, diagrams, practice problems on paper. The LMS doesn't capture someone downloading materials once and then studying them intensively for days. Low online activity doesn't mean low learning.
Q22: Logs can show patterns like when people access materials and how often they log in. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of attention during online time, and all the learning that happens with physical materials. They can't capture whether someone is actually understanding or just clicking through. They measure presence, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material until it makes sense, connecting new concepts to what I already know, and caring about actually understanding rather than just passing. For me, engagement looks like filling notebooks with diagrams and explanations in my own words. It's an internal state of curiosity and effort that no system can fully measure.
Q24: The LMS would never show how I transformed downloaded slides into elaborate hand-drawn visual summaries, or the hours I spent at the library with physical books. It wouldn't capture my resilience—how I kept pushing through despite anxiety, sadness, and financial stress. It wouldn't show the peer study sessions that happened offline, or how much the gamified elements actually lifted my spirits on hard days. My real engagement story is mostly invisible to any tracking system.
